column1: #FORMAT=WebAnno TSV 3.3
#T_SP=custom.Span|label
#Text=Sect.
#Text=I.
#Text=DIETETICS OF INFANCY.
#Text=In the early months of infancy the organs of digestion are unsuited to\r
#Text=any other food than that derived from the breast of the mother.
#Text=So\r
#Text=little capable are they, indeed, to digest any other, even of the\r
#Text=blandest and most digestible kind, that probably not more than one\r
#Text=infant in six or seven ever arrives at the more advanced periods of\r
#Text=life when deprived of the kind of nourishment nature intended for this\r
#Text=epoch.
#Text=It is not every parent, however, who is able to become a nurse; and\r
#Text=with many this office would not only be highly injurious to their own\r
#Text=health, but materially so to that of their offspring.
#Text=This may arise\r
#Text=from various causes, hereafter to be noticed, but whenever they exist a\r
#Text=wet-nurse is demanded.
#Text=Again, the latter resource is not always attainable, so that the\r
#Text=hazardous experiment of an artificial diet, or bringing up by hand, as\r
#Text=it is then termed, is obliged to be resorted to.
#Text=Thus, infantile dietetics naturally divides itself into Maternal\r
#Text=Nursing, Wet-Nurse Suckling, And Artificial Feeding.\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=1.
#Text=MATERNAL NURSING.
#Text=PLAN OF SUCKLING.
#Text=From the first moment the infant is applied to the breast, it must be\r
#Text=nursed upon a certain plan.
#Text=This is necessary to the well-doing of the\r
#Text=child, and will contribute essentially to preserve the health of the\r
#Text=parent, who will thus be rendered a good nurse, and her duty at the\r
#Text=same time will become a pleasure.
#Text=This implies, however, a careful attention on the part of the mother\r
#Text=to her own health; for that of her child is essentially dependent upon\r
#Text=it.
#Text=Healthy, nourishing, and digestible milk can be procured only from\r
#Text=a healthy parent; and it is against common sense to expect that, if a\r
#Text=mother impairs her health and digestion by improper diet, neglect\r
#Text=of exercise, and impure air, she can, nevertheless, provide as\r
#Text=wholesome and uncontaminated a fluid for her child, as if she were\r
#Text=diligently attentive to these important points.
#Text=Every instance of\r
#Text=indisposition in the nurse is liable to affect the infant.
#Text=And this leads me to observe, that it is a common mistake to suppose\r
#Text=that, because a woman is nursing, she ought therefore to live very\r
#Text=fully, and to add an allowance of wine, porter, or other fermented\r
#Text=liquor, to her usual diet.
#Text=The only result of this plan is, to cause an\r
#Text=unnatural degree of fulness in the system, which places the nurse on\r
#Text=the brink of disease, and which of itself frequently puts a stop to the\r
#Text=secretion of the milk, instead of increasing it.
#Text=The right plan of\r
#Text=proceeding is plain enough; only let attention be paid to the ordinary\r
#Text=laws of health, and the mother, if she have a sound constitution, will\r
#Text=make a better nurse than by any foolish deviation founded on ignorance\r
#Text=and caprice.
#Text=The following case proves the correctness of this statement:--\r
#Text=\r
#Text=A young married lady, confined with her first child, left the lying-in-\r
#Text=room at the expiration of the third week, a good nurse, and in perfect\r
#Text=health.
#Text=She had had some slight trouble with her nipples, but this was\r
#Text=soon overcome.
#Text=The porter system was now commenced, and from a pint to a pint and a\r
#Text=half of this beverage was taken in the four and twenty hours.
#Text=This was\r
#Text=resorted to, not because there was any deficiency in the supply of\r
#Text=milk, for it was ample, and the infant thriving upon it; but because,\r
#Text=having become a nurse, she was told that it was usual and necessary,\r
#Text=and that without it her milk and strength would ere long fail.
#Text=After this plan had been followed for a few days, the mother became\r
#Text=drowsy and disposed to sleep in the daytime; and headach, thirst, a hot\r
#Text=skin, in fact, fever supervened; the milk diminished in quantity, and,\r
#Text=for the first time, the stomach and bowels of the infant became\r
#Text=disordered.
#Text=The porter was ordered to be left off; remedial measures\r
#Text=were prescribed; and all symptoms, both in parent and child, were after\r
#Text=a while removed, and health restored.
#Text=Having been accustomed, prior to becoming a mother, to take a glass or\r
#Text=two of wine, and occasionally a tumbler of table beer, she was advised\r
#Text=to follow precisely her former dietetic plan, but with the addition of\r
#Text=half a pint of barley-milk morning and night.
#Text=Both parent and child\r
#Text=continued in excellent health during the remaining period of suckling,\r
#Text=and the latter did not taste artificial food until the ninth month, the\r
#Text=parent's milk being all-sufficient for its wants.
#Text=No one can doubt that the porter was in this case the source of the\r
#Text=mischief.
#Text=The patient had gone into the lying-in-room in full health,\r
#Text=had had a good time, and came out from her chamber (comparatively) as\r
#Text=strong as she entered it.
#Text=Her constitution had not been previously worn\r
#Text=down by repeated child-bearing and nursing, she had an ample supply of\r
#Text=milk, and was fully capable, therefore, of performing the duties which\r
#Text=now devolved upon her, without resorting to any unusual stimulant or\r
#Text=support.
#Text=Her previous habits were totally at variance with the plan\r
#Text=which was adopted; her system became too full, disease was produced,\r
#Text=and the result experienced was nothing more than what might be expected.
#Text=The plan to be followed for the first six months.
#Text=-Until the breast-\r
#Text=milk is fully established, which may not be until the second or third\r
#Text=day subsequent to delivery (almost invariably so in a first\r
#Text=confinement), the infant must be fed upon a little thin gruel, or upon\r
#Text=one third water and two thirds milk, sweetened with loaf sugar.
#Text=After this time it must obtain its nourishment from the breast alone,\r
#Text=and for a week or ten days the appetite of the infant must be the\r
#Text=mother's guide, as to the frequency in offering the breast.
#Text=The stomach\r
#Text=at birth is feeble, and as yet unaccustomed to food; its wants,\r
#Text=therefore, are easily satisfied, but they are frequently renewed.
#Text=An\r
#Text=interval, however, sufficient for digesting the little swallowed, is\r
#Text=obtained before the appetite again revives, and a fresh supply is\r
#Text=demanded.
#Text=At the expiration of a week or so it is essentially necessary, and\r
#Text=with some children this may be done with safety from the first day of\r
#Text=suckling, to nurse the infant at regular intervals of three or four\r
#Text=hours, day and night.
#Text=This allows sufficient time for each meal to be\r
#Text=digested, and tends to keep the bowels of the child in order.
#Text=Such\r
#Text=regularity, moreover, will do much to obviate fretfulness, and that\r
#Text=constant cry, which seems as if it could be allayed only by constantly\r
#Text=putting the child to the breast.
#Text=A young mother very frequently runs\r
#Text=into a serious error in this particular, considering every expression\r
#Text=of uneasiness as an indication of appetite, and whenever the infant\r
#Text=cries offering it the breast, although ten minutes may not have elapsed\r
#Text=since its last meal.
#Text=This is an injurious and even dangerous practice,\r
#Text=for, by overloading the stomach, the food remains undigested, the\r
#Text=child's bowels are always out of order, it soon becomes restless and\r
#Text=feverish, and is, perhaps, eventually lost; when, by simply attending\r
#Text=to the above rules of nursing, the infant might have become healthy and\r
#Text=vigorous.
#Text=For the same reason, the infant that sleeps with its parent must not\r
#Text=be allowed to have the nipple remaining in its mouth all night.
#Text=If\r
#Text=nursed as suggested, it will be found to awaken, as the hour for its\r
#Text=meal approaches, with great regularity.
#Text=In reference to night-nursing,\r
#Text=I would suggest suckling the babe as late as ten o'clock p. m., and not\r
#Text=putting it to the breast again until five o'clock the next morning.
#Text=Many mothers have adopted this hint, with great advantage to their own\r
#Text=health, and without the slightest detriment to that of the child.
#Text=With\r
#Text=the latter it soon becomes a habit; to induce it, however, it must be\r
#Text=taught early.
#Text=The foregoing plan, and without variation, must be pursued to the\r
#Text=sixth month.
#Text=AFTER THE SIXTH MONTH TO THE TIME OF WEANING.
#Text=--If the parent has a\r
#Text=large supply of good and nourishing milk, and her child is healthy and\r
#Text=evidently flourishing upon it, no change in its diet ought to be made.
#Text=If otherwise, however, (and this will but too frequently be the case,\r
#Text=even before the sixth month[FN#1],) the child may be fed twice in the\r
#Text=course of the day, and that kind of food chosen which, after a little\r
#Text=trial, is found to agree best.
#Text=Leman's tops and bottoms, steeped in hot water, with the addition of a\r
#Text=little fresh milk, and sweetened or not with loaf sugar, is one of the\r
#Text=best description.
#Text=If the stomach reject this, farinaceous food boiled in water, and\r
#Text=mixed with a small quantity of milk, may be employed.
#Text=Or weak mutton or\r
#Text=veal broth, or beef tea, clear and free from fat, and mixed with an\r
#Text=equal quantity of farinaceous food.
#Text=If this artificial diet is used before the sixth month, it must be\r
#Text=given through the sucking-bottle; after this period with a spoon: in\r
#Text=either case it must be previously passed through a sieve.
#Text=When the large or grinding teeth have appeared, the same food is still\r
#Text=to be continued, but need not any longer be expressed through the sieve.
#Text=Such is the plan of nursing to be followed by the mother until she\r
#Text=wean her infant altogether from the breast.
#Text=The period when this ought\r
#Text=to take place, as also the manner of accomplishing it, are detailed\r
#Text=in the section on "Weaning."
#Text=[FN#2]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=DEFICIENCY OF MILK.
#Text=If this deficiency exist from the earliest weeks after delivery, and\r
#Text=it is not quickly remedied by the means presently to be pointed out, a\r
#Text=wet-nurse must be obtained.
#Text=It will be of no avail partially to nurse,\r
#Text=and partially to feed the infant at this period and under such\r
#Text=circumstances, for if it is not soon lost, it will only live for a few\r
#Text=months, or a year at most, and be an object of the greatest anxiety and\r
#Text=grief to its parent.
#Text=This condition arises from the unwholesomeness of\r
#Text=the mother's milk, united with the artificial food; for when the milk\r
#Text=is deficient from the first, and continues so notwithstanding the means\r
#Text=used for its increase, it is invariably unhealthy in its quality.
#Text=This deficiency, however, may exist, and even at a very early period\r
#Text=after delivery, and yet be removed.
#Text=This, however, is not to be\r
#Text=accomplished by the means too frequently resorted to; for it is the\r
#Text=custom with many, two or three weeks after their confinement, if the\r
#Text=supply of nourishment for the infant is scanty, to partake largely of\r
#Text=malt liquor for its increase.
#Text=Sooner or later this will be found\r
#Text=injurious to the constitution of the mother: but how, then, is this\r
#Text=deficiency to be obviated?
#Text=Let the nurse keep but in good health, and\r
#Text=this point gained, the milk, both as to quantity and quality, will be\r
#Text=as ample, nutritious, and good, as can be produced by the individual.
#Text=I would recommend a plain, generous, and nutritious diet; not one\r
#Text=description of food exclusively, but, as is natural, a wholesome,\r
#Text=mixed, animal, and vegetable diet, with or without wine or malt liquor,\r
#Text=according to former habit; and, occasionally, where malt liquor has\r
#Text=never been previously taken, a pint of good sound ale may be taken\r
#Text=daily with advantage, if it agree with the stomach.
#Text=Regular exercise in\r
#Text=the open air is of the greatest importance, as it has an extraordinary\r
#Text=influence in promoting the secretion of healthy milk.
#Text=Early after\r
#Text=leaving the lying-in room, carriage exercise, where it can be\r
#Text=obtained, is to be preferred, to be exchanged, in a week or so, for\r
#Text=horse exercise, or the daily walk.
#Text=The tepid, or cold salt-water shower\r
#Text=bath, should be used every morning; but if it cannot be borne, sponging\r
#Text=the body withsalt-water must be substituted.
#Text=By adopting with perseverance the foregoing plan, a breast of milk\r
#Text=will be obtained as ample in quantity, and good in quality, as the\r
#Text=constitution of the parent can produce, as the following case proves:\r
#Text=\r
#Text=On the 17th September, 1839, I attended a lady twenty-four years of\r
#Text=age, a delicate, but healthy woman, in her first confinement.
#Text=The\r
#Text=labour was good.
#Text=Every thing went on well for the first week, except\r
#Text=that, although the breasts became enlarged, and promised a good supply\r
#Text=of nourishment for the infant, at its close there was merely a little\r
#Text=oozing from the nipple.
#Text=During the next fortnight a slight, but very\r
#Text=gradual increase in quantity took place, so that a dessert spoonful\r
#Text=only was obtained about the middle of this period, and perhaps double\r
#Text=this quantity at its expiration.
#Text=In the mean time the child was\r
#Text=necessarily fed upon an artificial diet, and as a consequence its\r
#Text=bowels became deranged, and a severe diarrhoea followed.
#Text=A wet-nurse\r
#Text=was advised for the child as the only means of saving its life, and\r
#Text=change of air for the mother as the most likely expedient (in\r
#Text=connection with the general treatment pointed out above) for obtaining\r
#Text=a good breast of milk.
#Text=Accordingly, on the 5th October, the patient,\r
#Text=taking with her the infant and a wet-nurse, went a few miles from town.
#Text=For three or four days it was a question whether the little one would\r
#Text=live, for so greatly had it been reduced by the looseness of the bowels\r
#Text=that it had not strength to grasp the nipple of its nurse; the milk,\r
#Text=therefore, was obliged to be drawn, and the child fed with it from a\r
#Text=spoon.
#Text=After the lapse of a few days, however, it could obtain the\r
#Text=breast-milk for itself; and, to make short of the case, on the 25th of\r
#Text=the same month, the mother and child returned home, the former having a\r
#Text=very fair proportion of healthy milk in her bosom, and the child\r
#Text=perfectly recovered and evidently thriving fast upon it.
#Text=Where, however, there has been an early deficiency in the supply of\r
#Text=nourishment, it will most frequently happen that, before the sixth or\r
#Text=seventh month, the infant's demands will be greater than the mother can\r
#Text=meet.
#Text=The deficiency must be made up by artificial food, which must be\r
#Text=of a kind generally employed before the sixth month, and given through\r
#Text=the bottle.
#Text=If, however, this plan of dieting should disagree, the\r
#Text=child must, even at this period, have a wet-nurse.
#Text=Women who marry comparatively late in life, and bear children,\r
#Text=generally have a deficiency of milk after the second or third month:\r
#Text=artificial feeding must in part be here resorted to.
#Text=THE INJURIOUS EFFECTS TO THE MOTHER AND INFANT OF UNDUE AND PROTRACTED\r
#Text=SUCKLING.
#Text=UPON THE MOTHER.
#Text=--The period of suckling is generally one of the most\r
#Text=healthy of a woman's life.
#Text=But there are exceptions to this as a\r
#Text=general rule; and nursing, instead of being accompanied by health, may\r
#Text=be the cause of its being materially, and even fatally, impaired.
#Text=This\r
#Text=may arise out of one of two causes, either, a parent continuing to\r
#Text=suckle too long; or, from the original powers or strength not being\r
#Text=equal to the continued drain on the system.
#Text=Examples of the first class I am meeting with daily.
#Text=I refer to poor\r
#Text=married women, who, having nursed their infants eighteen months, two\r
#Text=years, or even longer than this, from the belief that by so doing they\r
#Text=will prevent pregnancy, call to consult me with an exhausted frame and\r
#Text=disordered general health, arising solely from protracted nursing,\r
#Text=pursued from the above mistaken notion.
#Text=I most frequently meet with examples of the second class in the\r
#Text=delicate woman, who, having had two or three children in quick\r
#Text=succession, her health has given way, so that she has all the symptoms\r
#Text=arising from undue suckling, when perhaps the infant at her breast is\r
#Text=not more than two or three months old.
#Text=Since the health of the mother, then, will suffer materially from this\r
#Text=circumstance, she ought not to be ignorant of the fact; so that, when\r
#Text=the first symptoms manifest themselves, she may be able to recognise\r
#Text=their insidious approach; and tracing them to their real cause, obtain\r
#Text=medical advice before her health be seriously impaired.
#Text=SYMPTOMS.
#Text=--The earliest symptom is a dragging sensation in the back\r
#Text=when the child is in the act of sucking, and an exhausted feeling of\r
#Text=sinking and emptiness at the pit of the stomach afterwards.
#Text=This is\r
#Text=soon followed by loss of appetite, costive bowels, and pain on the left\r
#Text=side; then, the head will be more or less affected, sometimes with much\r
#Text=throbbing, singing in the ears, and always some degree of giddiness,\r
#Text=with great depression of spirits.
#Text=Soon the chest becomes affected, and the breathing is short,\r
#Text=accompanied by a dry cough and palpitation of the heart upon the\r
#Text=slightest exertion.
#Text=As the disease advances, the countenance becomes\r
#Text=very pale, and the flesh wastes, and profuse night perspirations, great\r
#Text=debility, swelling of the ankles, and nervousness ensue.
#Text=It is\r
#Text=unnecessary, however, to enter into a more full detail of symptoms.
#Text=TREATMENT.
#Text=--All that it will be useful to say in reference to\r
#Text=treatment, is this; that, although much may be done in the first\r
#Text=instance by medicine, change of air, cold and sea bathing, yet the\r
#Text=quickest and most effectual remedy is to wean the child, and thus\r
#Text=remove the cause.
#Text=THE ILL EFFECTS UPON THE INFANT.
#Text=--There is another and equally powerful\r
#Text=reason why the child should be weaned, or rather, have a young and\r
#Text=healthy wet-nurse, if practicable.
#Text=The effects upon the infant, suckled\r
#Text=under such circumstances, will be most serious.
#Text=Born in perfect health,\r
#Text=it will now begin to fall off in its appearance, for the mother's milk\r
#Text=will be no longer competent to afford it due nourishment; it will be\r
#Text=inadequate in quantity and quality.
#Text=Its countenance, therefore, will\r
#Text=become pale; its look sickly and aged; the flesh soft and flabby; the\r
#Text=limbs emaciated; the belly, in some cases, large, in others, shrunk;\r
#Text=and the evacuations fetid and unnatural; and in a very few weeks, the\r
#Text=blooming healthy child will be changed into the pale, sickly, peevish,\r
#Text=wasted creature, whose life appears hardly desirable.
#Text=The only measure that can save the life, and recover an infant from\r
#Text=this state, is that which would previously have prevented it a healthy\r
#Text=wet-nurse.
#Text=If the effects upon the infant should not be so aggravated as those\r
#Text=just described, and it subsequently live and thrive, there will be a\r
#Text=tendency in such a constitution to scrofula and consumption, to\r
#Text=manifest itself at some future period of life, undoubtedly acquired\r
#Text=from the parent, and dependent upon the impaired state of her health at\r
#Text=the time of its suckling.
#Text=A wet-nurse early resorted to, will prevent\r
#Text=this.
#Text=It will be naturally asked, for how long a period a mother ought to\r
#Text=perform the office of a nurse?
#Text=No specific time can be mentioned, and\r
#Text=the only way in which the question can be met is this: no woman, with\r
#Text=advantage to her own health, can suckle her infant beyond twelve or\r
#Text=eighteen months; and at various periods between the third and twelfth\r
#Text=month, many women will be obliged partially or entirely to resign the\r
#Text=office.
#Text=[FN#4]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[FN#4]  See "Weaning," p. 51.
#Text=The monthly periods generally reappear from the twelfth to the\r
#Text=fourteenth month from delivery; and when established, as the milk is\r
#Text=found invariably to diminish in quantity, and also to deteriorate in\r
#Text=quality, and the child is but imperfectly nourished, it is positively\r
#Text=necessary in such instances at once to wean it.
#Text=THE KIND OF ARTIFICIAL FOOD BEFORE THE SIXTH MONTH.
#Text=It should be as like the breast-milk as possible.
#Text=This is obtained by\r
#Text=a mixture of cow's milk, water, and sugar, in the following\r
#Text=proportions:--\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=Fresh cow's milk, two thirds;\r
#Text=Boiling water, or thin barley water, one third;\r
#Text=Loaf sugar, a sufficient quantity to sweeten.
#Text=This is the best diet that can be used for the first six months, after\r
#Text=which some farinaceous food may be combined.
#Text=In early infancy, mothers are too much in the habit of giving thick\r
#Text=gruel, panada, biscuit-powder, and such matters, thinking that a diet\r
#Text=of a lighter kind will not nourish.
#Text=This is a mistake; for these\r
#Text=preparations are much too solid; they overload the stomach, and cause\r
#Text=indigestion, flatulence, and griping.
#Text=These create a necessity for\r
#Text=purgative medicines and carminatives, which again weaken digestion,\r
#Text=and, by unnatural irritation, perpetuate the evils which render them\r
#Text=necessary.
#Text=Thus many infants are kept in a continual round of\r
#Text=repletion, indigestion, and purging, with the administration of\r
#Text=cordials and narcotics, who, if their diet were in quantity and quality\r
#Text=suited to their digestive powers, would need no aid from physic or\r
#Text=physicians.
#Text=In preparing this diet, it is highly important to obtain pure milk,\r
#Text=not previously skimmed, or mixed with water; and in warm weather just\r
#Text=taken from the cow.
#Text=It should not be mixed with the water or sugar\r
#Text=until wanted, and not more made than will be taken by the child at the\r
#Text=time, for it must be prepared fresh at every meal.
#Text=It is best not to\r
#Text=heat the milk over the fire, but let the water be in a boiling state\r
#Text=when mixed with it, and thus given to the infant tepid or lukewarm.
#Text=As the infant advances in age, the proportion of milk may be gradually\r
#Text=increased; this is necessary after the second month, when three parts\r
#Text=of milk to one of water may be allowed.
#Text=But there must be no change in\r
#Text=the kind of diet if the health of the child is good, and its appearance\r
#Text=perceptibly improving.
#Text=Nothing is more absurd than the notion, that in\r
#Text=early life children require a variety of food; only one kind of food is\r
#Text=prepared by nature, and it is impossible to transgress this law without\r
#Text=marked injury.
#Text=If cow's milk disagree with an infant--and this is sometimes\r
#Text=unfortunately the case, even from its birth ass's milk,--diluted with\r
#Text=one third its quantity of water, may be given as a substitute.
#Text=I am now\r
#Text=attending a lady in her fourth confinement, who is unable, from defect\r
#Text=in her nipples, to suckle her children.
#Text=The first child had a healthy\r
#Text=wet-nurse, and has grown a fine healthy lad.
#Text=The second, a girl, was\r
#Text=unfortunate in her nurse, she being of a strumous and unhealthy\r
#Text=constitution, although to a casual observer bearing the appearance of\r
#Text=health.
#Text=The child lived only three months, and the nurse died of a\r
#Text=rapid consumption shortly after.
#Text=This discouraged the mother from\r
#Text=adopting wet-nurse suckling for the third child (a great error); and an\r
#Text=artificial diet of cow's milk was resorted to.
#Text=The third day from\r
#Text=commencing this plan, flatulence, griping, purging, and vomiting came\r
#Text=on, one symptom quickly following the other; the child wasted, and on\r
#Text=the sixth day had several convulsive fits.
#Text=The diet was immediately\r
#Text=changed for ass's milk, and in less than twelve hours the sickness and\r
#Text=purging ceased; the flatulence was relieved; the motions, from being\r
#Text=green, watery, and passed with great violence and pain, became of a\r
#Text=healthy consistence and colour, and the screaming ceased.
#Text=The symptoms\r
#Text=did not return, the child thrived, very soon consuming regularly one\r
#Text=quart of the ass's milk daily, and is now a fine healthy girl two years\r
#Text=old.
#Text=A fortnight since the parent was confined with a fourth child.
#Text=Cow's milk was given to it for two or three days (from the difficulty\r
#Text=of obtaining that of the ass), the same train of symptoms, precisely,\r
#Text=came on with which the third child had been affected, which again gave\r
#Text=way upon following up the same plan of diet--the substitution of the\r
#Text=ass's milk for that of the cow.
#Text=The evident conclusion from this is,\r
#Text=that the breast-milk of a healthy woman is incomparably the most\r
#Text=suitable diet for the infant; but that, if she be not of a healthy\r
#Text=constitution, it may be destructive to the child; and that where this\r
#Text=cannot be obtained, and cow's milk is found to disagree, ass's milk may\r
#Text=sometimes be resorted to with the happiest results.
#Text=[FN#8]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=[FN#8]  An infant will generally consume a quart, or a little more, of\r
#Text=ass's milk in the four and twenty hours; and as this quantity is\r
#Text=nearly as much as the animal will give, it is best to purchase an ass\r
#Text=for the express purpose.
#Text=The foal must be separated from the mother,\r
#Text=and the forage of the latter carefully attended to, or the milk will\r
#Text=disagree with the child.
#Text=Sometimes the mother's breast, and every description of milk, is\r
#Text=rejected by the child; in which case recourse must be had to veal or\r
#Text=weak mutton broth, or beef tea, clear and free from fat, mixed with a\r
#Text=very small quantity of farinaceous food, carefully passed through a\r
#Text=sieve before it is poured into the sucking-bottle.
#Text=THE MODE OF ADMINISTERING IT.
#Text=--There are two ways--by the spoon, and by\r
#Text=the nursing-bottle.
#Text=The first ought never to be employed at this\r
#Text=period, inasmuch as the power of digestion in infants is very weak,\r
#Text=and their food is designed by nature to be taken very slowly into the\r
#Text=stomach, being procured from the breast by the act of sucking, in which\r
#Text=act a great quantity of saliva is secreted, and being poured into the\r
#Text=mouth, mixes with the milk, and is swallowed with it.
#Text=This process of\r
#Text=nature, then, should be emulated as far as possible; and food (for this\r
#Text=purpose) should be imbibed by suction from a nursing-bottle: it is thus\r
#Text=obtained slowly, and the suction employed secures the mixture of a due\r
#Text=quantity of saliva, which has a highly important influence on digestion.
#Text=Too much care cannot be taken to keep the bottle perfectly sweet.
#Text=For\r
#Text=this purpose there should always be two in the nursery, to be used\r
#Text=alternately; and, if any food remain after a meal, it must be emptied\r
#Text=out.
#Text=The bottle must always be scalded out after use.
#Text=The flat glass\r
#Text=nursing-bottle itself is too well known to need description; it may be\r
#Text=well, however, to say a word about the teat that covers its narrow\r
#Text=neck, and through which the infant sucks the food.
#Text=If the artificial\r
#Text=or prepared cow's teat is made use of, it should be so attached to the\r
#Text=bottle that its extremity does not extend beyond its apex more than\r
#Text=half or three quarters of an inch; for if it projects more than this,\r
#Text=the child will get the sides of the teat so firmly pressed together\r
#Text=between its gums, that there will be no channel for the milk to flow\r
#Text=through.
#Text=This remark applies equally to the teat made of soft wash-\r
#Text=leather, which many ladies prefer to that of the cow, and it is a good\r
#Text=substitute; but then a fresh piece of leather must be made use of\r
#Text=daily, otherwise the food will be tainted, and the child's bowels\r
#Text=deranged.
#Text=It is also necessary that both of these, when used, should\r
#Text=have a small conical piece of sponge inclosed.
#Text=The most cleanly and convenient apparatus is a cork nipple, upon the\r
#Text=plan of M.
#Text=Darbo, of Paris, fixed in the sucking-bottle.
#Text=[FN#9] The cork,\r
#Text=being of a particularly fine texture, is supple and elastic, yielding\r
#Text=to the infant's lips while sucking, and is much more durable than the\r
#Text=teats ordinarily used.
#Text=Whatever kind of bottle or teat is used, however, it must never be\r
#Text=forgotten that cleanliness is absolutely essential to the success of\r
#Text=this plan of rearing children.
#Text=THE QUANTITY OF FOOD TO BE GIVEN AT EACH MEAL.
#Text=--This must be regulated\r
#Text=by the age of the child, and its digestive power.
#Text=A little experience\r
#Text=will soon enable a careful and observing mother to determine this\r
#Text=point.
#Text=--As the child grows older the quantity of course must be\r
#Text=increased.
#Text=The chief error in rearing the young is overfeeding; and a most\r
#Text=serious one it is; but which may be easily avoided by the parent\r
#Text=pursuing a systematic plan with regard to the hours of feeding, and\r
#Text=then only yielding to the indications of appetite, and administering\r
#Text=the food slowly, in small quantities at a time.
#Text=This is the only way\r
#Text=effectually to prevent indigestion, and bowel complaints, and the\r
#Text=irritable condition of the nervous system, so common in infancy, and\r
#Text=secure to the infant healthy nutrition, and consequent strength of\r
#Text=constitution.
#Text=As has been well observed, "Nature never intended the\r
#Text=infant's stomach to be converted into a receptacle for laxatives,\r
#Text=carminatives, antacids, stimulants, and astringents; and when these\r
#Text=become necessary, we may rest assured that there is something faulty in\r
#Text=our management, however perfect it may seem to ourselves."
#Text=THE FREQUENCY OF GIVING FOOD.
#Text=--This must be determined, as a general\r
#Text=rule, by allowing such an interval between each meal as will insure the\r
#Text=digestion of the previous quantity; and this may be fixed at about\r
#Text=every three or four hours.
#Text=If this rule be departed from, and the child\r
#Text=receives a fresh supply of food every hour or so, time will not be\r
#Text=given for the digestion of the previous quantity, and as a consequence\r
#Text=of this process being interrupted, the food passing on into the bowel\r
#Text=undigested, will there ferment and become sour, will inevitably produce\r
#Text=cholic and purging, and in no way contribute to the nourishment of the\r
#Text=child.
#Text=THE POSTURE OF THE CHILD WHEN FED.
#Text=--It is important to attend to this.
#Text=It must not receive its meals lying; the head should be raised on the\r
#Text=nurse's arm, the most natural position, and one in which there will be\r
#Text=no danger of the food going the wrong way, as it is called.
#Text=After each\r
#Text=meal the little one should be put into its cot, or repose on its\r
#Text=mother's knee, for at least half an hour.
#Text=This is essential for the\r
#Text=process of digestion, as exercise is important at other times for the\r
#Text=promotion of health.
#Text=THE KIND OF ARTIFICIAL FOOD AFTER THE SIXTH MONTH, TO THE COMPLETION\r
#Text=OF FIRST DENTITION.
#Text=As soon as the child has got any teeth,--and about this period one or\r
#Text=two will make their appearance,--solid farinaceous matter boiled in\r
#Text=water, beaten through a sieve, and mixed with a small quantity of milk,\r
#Text=may be employed.
#Text=Or tops and bottoms, steeped in hot water, with the\r
#Text=addition of fresh milk and loaf sugar to sweeten.
#Text=And the child may\r
#Text=now, for the first time, be fed with a spoon.
#Text=When one or two of the large grinding teeth have appeared, the same\r
#Text=food may be continued, but need not be passed through a sieve.
#Text=Beef tea\r
#Text=and chicken broth may occasionally be added; and, as an introduction to\r
#Text=the use of a more completely animal diet, a portion, now and then, of a\r
#Text=soft boiled egg; by and by a small bread pudding, made with one egg in\r
#Text=it, may be taken as the dinner meal.
#Text=Nothing is more common than for parents during this period to give\r
#Text=their children animal food.
#Text=This is a great error.
#Text="To feed an infant\r
#Text=with animal food before it has teeth proper for masticating it, shows a\r
#Text=total disregard to the plain indications of nature, in withholding such\r
#Text=teeth till the system requires their assistance to masticate solid\r
#Text=food.

column3:
_	

#Text=[FN#10]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=If the principles already laid down be true, it cannot reasonably be\r

column1: #Text=maintained that a child's mouth without teeth, and that of an adult,\r
#Text=furnished with the teeth of carnivorous and graminivorous animals, are\r
#Text=designed by the Creator for the same sort of food.
#Text=most nourishment in itself, but from that which is best adapted to the\r
#Text=condition of the digestive organs at the time when it is taken."
#Text=SUGAR.
#Text=--This is a necessary condiment for the food of children, and it\r
#Text=is nutritious, and does not injure the teeth, as is generally imagined.
#Text="During the sugar season," observes Dr.
#Text=Dunglison, "the negroes of\r
#Text=the West India islands drink copiously of the juice of the cane, yet\r
#Text=their teeth are not injured; on the contrary, they have been praised by\r
#Text=writers for their beauty and soundness; and the rounded form of the\r
#Text=body, whilst they can indulge in the juice, sufficiently testifies to\r
#Text=the nutrient qualities of the saccharine beverage."
#Text=[FN#13] Sweetmeats,\r
#Text=on the other hand, are most indigestible, and seriously injurious.
#Text=SALT.
#Text=--This is necessary for the health of a child; it acts as a\r
#Text=stimulant to the digestive organs, and if not allowed in sufficient\r
#Text=quantity with the food, worms will result.
#Text=[FN#14] It may, therefore, be\r
#Text=added in small quantity, and with advantage, even to the farinaceous\r
#Text=food of infants.
#Text=Salted meats, however, should never be permitted to\r
#Text=the child; for by the process of salting the fibre of the meat is so\r
#Text=changed, that it is less nutritive, as well as less digestible.
#Text=FRUITS.
#Text=--These, and of all kinds whether fresh or dried, a delicate\r
#Text=child is better without; except the orange, which when perfectly ripe\r
#Text=may be allowed to any child, but the white or inner skin should be\r
#Text=scrupulously rejected, as it is most indigestible.
#Text=A healthy child may be permitted to partake of most fresh fruits.
#Text=Of\r
#Text=the stone-fruits, the ripe peach, the apricot, and nectarine, are the\r
#Text=most wholesome; but cherries, from the stones being but too frequently\r
#Text=swallowed, had better not be allowed.
#Text=Apples and pears, when ripe and\r
#Text=well masticated, are not unwholesome; and the apple when baked affords\r
#Text=a pleasant repast, and where there is a costive habit, it is useful as\r
#Text=a laxative.
#Text=The small-seeded fruits, however, are by far the most\r
#Text=wholesome.
#Text=Of these, the ripe strawberry and raspberry deserve the\r
#Text=first rank.
#Text=The grape is also cooling and antiseptic, but the husks and\r
#Text=seeds should be rejected.
#Text=The gooseberry is less wholesome on account\r
#Text=of the indigestibility of the skin, which is too frequently swallowed.
#Text=Dried fruits a child should never be permitted to eat.
#Text=WATER.
#Text=--This should be the only beverage throughout childhood.
#Text=Toast-\r
#Text=and-water, if the child prefer it, which is rendered slightly more\r
#Text=nutritive than the more simple fluid.
#Text=The water employed in its\r
#Text=preparation, however, must be at a boiling temperature, and it ought to\r
#Text=be drunk as soon as it has sufficiently cooled; for by being kept, it\r
#Text=acquires a mawkish and unpleasant flavour.
#Text=WINE, BEER, etc.
#Text=--The practice of giving wine, or, indeed, any\r
#Text=stimulant, to a healthy child, is highly reprehensible; it ought never\r
#Text=to be given but medicinally.
#Text=The circulation in infancy and childhood is not only more rapid than\r
#Text=in the adult, but easily excited to greater vehemence of action; the\r
#Text=nervous system, too, is so susceptible, that the slightest causes of\r
#Text=irritation produce strong and powerful impressions: the result in\r
#Text=either case is diseased action in the frame, productive of fever,\r
#Text=convulsions, etc.; wine, accordingly, is detrimental to children.
#Text=An experiment made by Dr.
#Text=Hunter upon two of his children illustrates,\r
#Text=in a striking manner, the pernicious effects of even a small portion of\r
#Text=intoxicating liquors in persons of this tender age.
#Text=To one of the\r
#Text=children he gave, every day after dinner, a full glass of sherry: the\r
#Text=child was five years of age, and unaccustomed to the use of wine.
#Text=To\r
#Text=the other child, of nearly the same age, and equally unused to wine, he\r
#Text=gave an orange.
#Text=In the course of a week, a very marked difference was\r
#Text=perceptible in the pulse, urine, and evacuations from the bowels of the\r
#Text=two children.
#Text=The pulse of the first was raised, the urine high\r
#Text=coloured, and the evacuations destitute of their usual quantity of\r
#Text=bile.
#Text=In the other child, no change whatever was produced.
#Text=He then\r
#Text=reversed the experiment, giving to the first the orange, and to the\r
#Text=second the wine, and the results corresponded: the child who had the\r
#Text=orange continued well, and the system of the other got straightway\r
#Text=into disorder, as in the first experiment.
#Text=[FN#15]\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=In this town, spirits, particularly gin, are given to infants and\r
#Text=children to a frightful extent.
#Text=I have seen an old Irish woman give\r
#Text=diluted spirits to the infant just born.
#Text=A short time since one of\r
#Text=those dram-drinking children, about eight years of age, was brought\r
#Text=into one of our hospitals.
#Text=The attendants, from its emaciated\r
#Text=appearance, considered the child was dying from mere starvation; which\r
#Text=was true enough in a certain sense.
#Text=Food was accordingly offered and\r
#Text=pressed upon it, but the boy would not even put it to his lips.
#Text=The\r
#Text=next day it was discovered that the mother brought the child very\r
#Text=nearly a pint of gin, every drop of which before night he had consumed.
#Text=It is easy to discover when children have been fed upon spirits: they\r
#Text=are always emaciated; have a lean, yellow, haggard look: the eyes\r
#Text=sunk, the lips pale, and the teeth discoloured, the cadaverous aspect\r
#Text=of the countenance being most fearful.
#Text=They are continually suffering\r
#Text=from bowel complaints and convulsive disorders; which, under these\r
#Text=circumstances, terminate invariably in an early death.
#Text=Sect.
#Text=V.
#Text=--BLISTERS AND POULTICES.
#Text=A blister should never be applied for any infantile disease, except\r
#Text=when ordered by a medical man, as its injudicious use might greatly\r
#Text=aggravate the complaint.
#Text=There are also one or two precautions in reference to the mode of the\r
#Text=application of a blister, which it is always right for a parent to\r
#Text=attend to.
#Text=From the great irritability of the skin, it should never be\r
#Text=allowed to remain on longer than from two to four hours.
#Text=At the\r
#Text=expiration of this time, the surface will usually become red and\r
#Text=inflamed; and, if the blister is removed, and the part dressed with\r
#Text=fresh spermaceti ointment spread on lint, or with a soft bread and\r
#Text=water poultice, a full blister will soon be raised: the little patient\r
#Text=is thus saved much suffering, and a very troublesome sore prevented.
#Text=A\r
#Text=piece of tissue or silver paper, interposed between the blister and the\r
#Text=skin, will answer the same purpose; the blister will act well, and the\r
#Text=evils before alluded to will be prevented.
#Text=After a blister has been two or three hours applied, its edge should\r
#Text=be carefully raised, to ascertain the effect produced; and if the\r
#Text=surface be much inflamed, more particularly if little points of\r
#Text=vesication (watery bladders) are present, it should be removed, and the\r
#Text=above directions attended to.
#Text=Mustard poultices are invaluable in some of the diseases of infancy\r
#Text=and childhood, and therefore frequently ordered.
#Text=A mustard poultice is made by mixing two thirds of mustard flour and\r
#Text=one third of wheaten flour with warm water or vinegar, in sufficient\r
#Text=quantity to render the powder of the consistence of paste.
#Text=It is then\r
#Text=spread on linen from the size of a half-crown to that of the palm of\r
#Text=the hand, according to the effect intended, and placed on the skin.
#Text=How\r
#Text=long it is to be kept on will depend upon the individual sensibility of\r
#Text=the skin of the child; but, in general, from fifteen to twenty minutes\r
#Text=will be found amply sufficient.
#Text=The application, however, must at all\r
#Text=times be carefully watched; for if it remain on too long, ulceration,\r
#Text=and death of the part, might ensue; therefore, directly the skin is\r
#Text=found tolerably red, the poultice should be removed.
#Text=After its removal,\r
#Text=the part may be exposed, or, if very painful, smeared over with fresh\r
#Text=cream or common cerate.
#Text=A bread and water poultice, although one of the commonest applications\r
#Text=in use, is rarely well made or properly applied.
#Text=It thus becomes\r
#Text=injurious rather than useful; adding to the inflammation or irritation\r
#Text=of the part, instead of soothing and allaying it.
#Text=Nothing, however, is\r
#Text=more simple than the mode of its preparation.
#Text=Cut slices of stale bread of sufficient quantity, scald out a bason,\r
#Text=put the bread into it, pour upon it boiling water, cover it over, and\r
#Text=let it stand for ten minutes; next strain the water oft, gently squeeze\r
#Text=the saturated bread in a thin cloth, so that the poultice shall not be\r
#Text=too moist, and then spread it upon a cloth so that it shall be in\r
#Text=thickness half an inch, and of a size large enough to cover the whole\r
#Text=of the inflamed part, and a little more.
#Text=Apply it just warm enough to\r
#Text=be borne, and cover it well with oiled silk.
#Text=A poultice thus made, will\r
#Text=act as a local tepid bath to the inflamed part; and the oiled silk\r
#Text=preventing evaporation, it will be found, when taken off, as moist as\r
#Text=the first moment that it was put on.
#Text=THE MANAGEMENT OF THE INFANT IN DIFFICULT TEETHING.
#Text=In the child which has been partly or altogether brought up by hand,\r
#Text=or who is of a feeble and delicate constitution, or imbued with any\r
#Text=hereditary taint, the process of dentition will be attended with more\r
#Text=or less difficulty, and not unfrequendy with danger.
#Text=SYMPTOMS.
#Text=--The symptoms of difficult dentition are of a much more\r
#Text=aggravated description than those which attend the former case; and it\r
#Text=is right that a mother should, to a certain extent, be acquainted\r
#Text=with their character, that she may early request that medical aid,\r
#Text=which, if judiciously applied, will mitigate, and generally quickly\r
#Text=remove them.
#Text=Difficult dentition will be attended with painful inflammation and\r
#Text=swelling of the gum, which is hotter, of a deeper red, than natural,\r
#Text=and intolerant of the slightest pressure.
#Text=There is often great\r
#Text=determination of blood to the head, which a mother may recognise by the\r
#Text=cheeks being red, hot, and swollen; the eyes red, irritable, and\r
#Text=watery; and the saliva running from the mouth profusely.
#Text=The fever is\r
#Text=great, and the thirst extreme.
#Text=The child is at one time restless and\r
#Text=irritable, and at another heavy and oppressed: the sleep will be\r
#Text=broken, and the infant frequently awake suddenly and in alarm from its\r
#Text=short slumbers.
#Text=Such are the chief symptoms of difficult teething, and\r
#Text=which will be present to a greater or less degree.
#Text=TREATMENT.
#Text=--As most of the above symptoms are induced by the painful\r
#Text=tension of the gum, it would seem that the most rational mode of\r
#Text=attempting their relief is by freely lancing the swollen part.
#Text=Great\r
#Text=prejudices, however, still exist in the minds of some parents against\r
#Text=this operation.
#Text=They think it gives great pain, and, if the tooth is\r
#Text=not very near, makes its coming through the gum subsequently the more\r
#Text=difficult.
#Text=With regard to the first objection, the lancet is carried through the\r
#Text=gum so quickly, that this is hardly possible; and the fact that the\r
#Text=infant will often smile in your face after it is done, although\r
#Text=previously crying from pain, is sufficient evidence that it is not a\r
#Text=very painful operation.
#Text=In reference to the second, that the scar which\r
#Text=ensues, opposes, by its hardness, the subsequent progress of the tooth,\r
#Text=it is quite groundless; for cicatrices, like all other new-formed\r
#Text=parts, are much more easily absorbed than the original structure.
#Text=Of the practical utility and perfect safety of this operation we have\r
#Text=ample proof in its daily performance with impunity, and in the instant\r
#Text=relief which it often affords to all the symptoms.
#Text=Mere scarifying the gums is sometimes all that is required, and will\r
#Text=afford great relief.
#Text=This operation, therefore, should not be opposed\r
#Text=by the mother.
#Text=She, at the same time, should be acquainted with its\r
#Text=precise object, lest the speedy return of the symptoms, and the non-\r
#Text=appearance of the expected tooth, might tend to bring the operation of\r
#Text=lancing the gums into disrepute.
#Text=The parental management of the infant, then, and by which much of the\r
#Text=pain and difficulty of teething may be removed or alleviated, consists\r
#Text=in attending to the following directions:--\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=FIRST, TO THE STATE OF THE MOUTH.
#Text=--To this it is an important part of\r
#Text=the mother's duty to pay especial attention; and, by so doing, she will\r
#Text=save her child much suffering.
#Text=The condition of the mouth should be\r
#Text=carefully inspected from time to time; and should a swollen gum be\r
#Text=discovered, it should immediately be attended to, not waiting till\r
#Text=constitutional symptoms appear before she employs proper aid for her\r
#Text=child.
#Text=For this purpose the mother should make herself familiar with\r
#Text=the appearances of the gum under distention and inflammation; a matter\r
#Text=of no difficulty, accompanied, as this condition usually is, by a\r
#Text=profuse secretion of saliva, heat of mouth, and at a time when the age\r
#Text=of the child justifies the supposition that it is about to cut its\r
#Text=first tooth, or, if it have some teeth, that others are about to appear.
#Text=SECONDLY, TO THE FOOD.
#Text=--If a child is teething with difficulty, it\r
#Text=should always have its quantity of nourishment diminished.
#Text=If it is\r
#Text=being fed, as well as nursed at the breast, at the time, the former\r
#Text=should be immediately withheld: if it is being fed alone, the only\r
#Text=kind of food that should be allowed is milk and water.
#Text=These cases are\r
#Text=much aggravated by the not uncommon habit of parents giving the infant\r
#Text=food whenever it cries from the irritation attending upon the process;\r
#Text=and thus a slightly difficult dentition is converted into serious\r
#Text=disease.
#Text=THIRDLY, TO THE STATE OF THE BOWELS.
#Text=--These must be carefully watched,\r
#Text=that they may not become confined; it being necessary that they should\r
#Text=be gently relaxed at this time.
#Text=If a slight diarrhoea is present, it\r
#Text=must not be checked; if it pass beyond this, however, medicine must be\r
#Text=had recourse to, and great benefit will also arise from putting the\r
#Text=child into a warm hip-bath, and warmly clothing the body, but keeping\r
#Text=the head cool.
#Text=FOURTHLY, TO THE HEAD.
#Text=--The infant's head should be washed with cold\r
#Text=water night and morning, and no other covering than that which nature\r
#Text=has provided should be put upon it when within doors or asleep; and on\r
#Text=no occasion should warm felt or velvet hats be worn during mild or warm\r
#Text=weather, straw or white hats being much lighter and cooler.
#Text=The child\r
#Text=should be much in the open air.
#Text=The sponging of the infant's body daily, either with cold or tepid\r
#Text=water, must depend upon the season of the year and constitution of the\r
#Text=child, as well as upon other circumstances.
#Text=Sponging the head with cold\r
#Text=water night and morning is almost invariably atttended with great\r
#Text=benefit, and may be resorted to in every case without fear; and now and\r
#Text=then the use of the warm hip-bath, for several days together, will be\r
#Text=ordered by the physician, which, by acting upon the skin, diminishes\r
#Text=the determination of blood to the head, and thus forms an important\r
#Text=source of relief.
#Text=FIFTHLY, OF CONVULSIONS.
#Text=--If they should occur, and they are not\r
#Text=unfrequently excited by difficult teething, and then give great alarm\r
#Text=to the parent, relief will be afforded by immersing the hips, legs,\r
#Text=and feet of the infant in water as warm as can be borne, and at the\r
#Text=same time applying over the head and temples a piece of flannel wet\r
#Text=with cold water.
#Text=I have also often cut the fit short by sprinkling cold\r
#Text=water in the child's face while in the bath.
#Text=The gums should always be\r
#Text=looked to, and if they appear swollen, and painful, at once lanced.
#Text=I\r
#Text=have known the most formidable convulsions to cease immediately after\r
#Text=this operation.
#Text=SIXTHLY, OF THE USE OF OPIATES.
#Text=--It is the practice with some nurses\r
#Text=to administer narcotics to quiet infants while teething.
#Text=It is not only\r
#Text=objectionable, but, from the uncertain effects of sedatives upon\r
#Text=infants, a very dangerous practice, and they ought never to be given,\r
#Text=except at the suggestion of a medical man.
#Text=It is far better, if the\r
#Text=child is restless at night, to have it frequently taken out of its cot,\r
#Text=and carried about in an airy room; for the cool air, and change of\r
#Text=posture, will do much to allay the feverishness and restlessness of the\r
#Text=child.
#Text=From these few hints, it must have been seen how much the sufferings\r
#Text=from teething may be mitigated by judicious management.
#Text=That, if the\r
#Text=parent is able to support her infant upon the breast alone, teething\r
#Text=will be found comparatively an easy process, and unattended with\r
#Text=danger; the mother thus reaping a delightful reward for all the\r
#Text=anxieties and privations nursing necessarily involves.
#Text=That the child\r
#Text=brought up partially, or entirely, by hand will always pass through\r
#Text=dentition with more or less of pain and difficulty; but that even here,\r
#Text=if the diet has been properly regulated, much less suffering and\r
#Text=inconvenience will arise than when less attention has been paid to it.
#Text=And, lastly, that, when teething is difficult, how highly important it\r
#Text=is to call in proper aid at an early period, and to carry out fully the\r
#Text=directions of the medical attendant, allowing no foolish prejudices to\r
#Text=interfere with his prescriptions and management.
#Text=If I stood in need of any argument to impress upon the mind of a\r
#Text=parent the importance of attending to the last injunction, I would\r
#Text=simply state, that its neglect is but too frequently the cause of\r
#Text=disease of the brain, terminating in death, or a state of idiotcy far\r
#Text=worse than death, of which I know more than one living instance.
#Text=It may be as well to add, that eruptions about the ears, head, face,\r
#Text=and various parts of the body, very frequently appear during the\r
#Text=process of the first teething.
#Text=[FN#23] If they are slight, they should\r
#Text=be left alone, being rather useful than otherwise; if they are\r
#Text=troublesome, they must receive that kind of attention from the parent\r
#Text=which will be pointed out under the chapter on diseases.
#Text=The same\r
#Text=remark applies to enlargements of the glands of the neck, which\r
#Text=frequently appear at this time.
#Text=[FN#23]  In some infants a rash always precedes the cutting a tooth.
#Text=Sometimes it appears in the form of hard elevated pimples as large as\r
#Text=peas; in other instances in the form of red patches, of the size of a\r
#Text=shilling, upon the arms, shoulders, and back of the neck.
#Text=They are\r
#Text=always harmless, require no particular attention, and prevent, I doubt\r
#Text=not, more serious complaints.
#Text=MATERNAL TREATMENT OF THE DISORDERS OF THE STOMACH AND BOWELS.
#Text=As must have been already seen, the maternal treatment chiefly\r
#Text=consists in the removal of the cause of the disorder; medicine may\r
#Text=occasionally be exhibited by the mother, but its use in her hands must\r
#Text=be very limited indeed.
#Text=Unfortunately the general resource and only remedy of most mothers in\r
#Text=affections of the stomach and bowels is an aperient, and a combination\r
#Text=containing calomel is the one too frequently selected.
#Text=The primary\r
#Text=cause of the disorder is undetected, and consequently no measures taken\r
#Text=for its removal, but purgative powder after purgative powder is given,\r
#Text=the evil being supposed to rest in the bowels alone, and that such\r
#Text=means must eventually get rid of it.
#Text=The mother is not aware all this\r
#Text=time that the real source of the derangement is probably in the diet\r
#Text=itself; that there is some error here, and that unless this is\r
#Text=corrected, the remedies must be worse than useless.
#Text=The consequence of\r
#Text=such a plan of proceeding is usually very sad; a confirmed and\r
#Text=obstinate diarrhoea but too commonly ensues, and the infant is\r
#Text=sometimes reduced to the last extremity.
#Text=The removal of the cause of the disorder, then, in a large number of\r
#Text=instances of derangement of the stomach and bowels, if effected early,\r
#Text=will cure the disease, and without further remedy.
#Text=But it will be\r
#Text=asked, by what method is this cause to be detected?
#Text=In this way.
#Text=In all\r
#Text=human probability the primary cause of the disorder is connected with\r
#Text=the diet; this is the case in ninety-nine instances out of a hundred.
#Text=Well, then, is the sick child at the breast?
#Text=If so, ascertain whether\r
#Text=the breast-milk is healthy and wholesome, or whether any circumstances\r
#Text=exist which have rendered it otherwise?
#Text=If nothing faulty is found\r
#Text=here, the next question would naturally be, whether the rules and\r
#Text=regulations laid down for suckling have been strictly adhered to?
#Text=Or,\r
#Text=whether the infant is sufficiently old to render it at all probable\r
#Text=that a tooth may be irritating the gum?
#Text=Perhaps the child is being weaned; well, is there any error here?
#Text=Is\r
#Text=the change being attempted too early?
#Text=or too suddenly and abruptly?
#Text=If\r
#Text=this is not the case, then, has the child been overfed, or is the food\r
#Text=given of the proper description?
#Text=Is the child being brought up by hand?
#Text=Then, there is every reason to\r
#Text=suspect, either that the quality of the food given is not the most\r
#Text=suitable, or, that the quantity exhibited is too great; in fact, that\r
#Text=the rules laid down for "artificial feeding" have not been strictly\r
#Text=acted upon.
#Text=By a mode of investigation like this, any defect or error in the\r
#Text=dietetic management of the infant producing the disorder will be easily\r
#Text=detected by a careful mother; and its correction alone will, in very\r
#Text=many instances, be all that is necessary to remove the symptoms.
#Text=For example, if flatulence and griping, followed by diarrhoea, occur\r
#Text=to an infant at the breast; if at the same time it becomes pale, its\r
#Text=flesh flabby, its disposition fretful, always crying until it is put to\r
#Text=the breast, the nipple of which it grasps eagerly, sucking eagerly, yet\r
#Text=never satisfied, for its hunger continues, it is not nourished; if,\r
#Text=too, the more it sucks, the more the stomach and bowels are deranged,\r
#Text=the more it vomits and is purged; depend upon it the cause of all the\r
#Text=evil will be found to be unwholesome milk.
#Text=No medicine will avail any\r
#Text=thing here; the cause must be removed; the best medicine, and the only\r
#Text=remedy, is a breast of healthy milk.
#Text=And if this is not procured\r
#Text=early, there will be great danger of a diarrhoea setting in, which may\r
#Text=probably prove fatal to the child.
#Text=Again; if there is simply vomiting of the breast-milk almost\r
#Text=immediately after the child has been suckled, the milk coming up pure\r
#Text=and unchanged, and discharged without any apparent effort, and the\r
#Text=moment after the child is cheerful and happy, this will be found to\r
#Text=depend upon repletion, and not upon unwholesome milk; in fact, the\r
#Text=stomach has received too much.
#Text=This must be prevented in future, not by\r
#Text=giving medicine, but simply by removing the infant from the nipple\r
#Text=immediately it ceases to draw strongly, the moment it begins to dally\r
#Text=with the breast.
#Text=Again; if flatulence and griping occur to the child brought up by\r
#Text=handy this derangement will generally be found to result from\r
#Text=overfeeding: abstinence and diminution of the quantity of the food will\r
#Text=generally be all that is necessary here.
#Text=It will be well, however, for\r
#Text=the mother in this case, and she may do it with the utmost safety, to\r
#Text=unload the bowels of their indigestible contents by the exhibition of a\r
#Text=tea-spoonful of castor oil.
#Text=A dose or two of this medicine will\r
#Text=effectually clear them out, without increasing the irritation, or\r
#Text=weakening the child, whilst it will in most instances altogether remove\r
#Text=the symptoms.
#Text=If the flatulence, however, should continue, four or five\r
#Text=grains of magnesia may be mixed with the last meal at night, and a\r
#Text=little warm water thrown up into the bowel as an injection the next\r
#Text=morning.
#Text=Diarrhoea occurring in a child brought up by hand, if it be not the\r
#Text=result of overfeeding, will very frequently be found to arise from\r
#Text=unsuitable diet, the food given not being of a kind suited to the\r
#Text=infant's stomach; for what will agree with one child often disagrees\r
#Text=with another.
#Text=Alteration of diet will sometimes alone suffice in these\r
#Text=cases to cure, if this alteration is only made early enough, before any\r
#Text=considerable irritation of the stomach and bowels has been induced.
#Text=Thin arrow-root made with water (prepared very carefully, or the child\r
#Text=will refuse it,) should be given for five or six days; the warm bath\r
#Text=used every night for the same period, a new flannel bandage rolled\r
#Text=round the body, and the child cautiously protected from a damp\r
#Text=atmosphere.
#Text=The arrow-root, upon the cessation of the diarrhoea, may\r
#Text=have cows' milk added to it, if milk is not found to disagree: when\r
#Text=this is the case, chicken or weak mutton broth, free from fat, or beef-\r
#Text=tea, thickened with farinaceous food, with a little salt added, are the\r
#Text=best substitutes.
#Text=Should not the diarrhoea yield to the foregoing\r
#Text=measures, and that readily, medical aid ought to be sought.
#Text=Diarrhoea\r
#Text=is very frequent from the time of weaning to the third year of age, and\r
#Text=certainly in its effects forms so important a disease, that, unless in\r
#Text=the slight form noticed above, a mother is not justified in attempting\r
#Text=its relief.
#Text=In conclusion, I would observe, that I do not think a mother justified\r
#Text=in attempting more than what has been laid down here for her guidance.
#Text=It is believed that the few and plain common-sense directions given, if\r
#Text=followed, will do much to prevent disease, and even to relieve it in\r
#Text=its milder forms; they will not, however, cure disease itself when\r
#Text=really established: and again I would repeat, let the mother recollect\r
#Text=that to prevent disease is her province--to cure it, is the physician's.
#Text=GARGLES AND THE INHALATION OF WARM WATER.
#Text=--When the throat is affected,\r
#Text=gargles are sometimes ordered; but the pain and inconvenience which\r
#Text=their employment gives rise to, frequently precludes their use: and\r
#Text=children seldom understand how to employ them, even if the state of the\r
#Text=throat permitted it.
#Text=Under these circumstances, the inhalation of the\r
#Text=steam of hot water, or hot vinegar and water, may be substituted, and\r
#Text=with decided benefit.
#Text=Mudge's inhaler is a good contrivance to effect\r
#Text=this.
#Text=When the throat is found by the mother to be early affected, an\r
#Text=immediate application to the medical adviser is especially important.
#Text=For, if he be called upon to treat this form of scarlet fever at its\r
#Text=very commencement, by judicious treatment, the duration and violence of\r
#Text=the disease may be both shortened and greatly mitigated.
#Text=REGIMEN.
#Text=--Cooling drinks, as plain water, toast and water, barley water\r
#Text=flavoured with lemon peel, fresh whey, lemonade, and thin gruel, may\r
#Text=all be resorted to in their turn.
#Text=The child may also be allowed\r
#Text=oranges, grapes, or lemons sweetened with sugar, particularly when the\r
#Text=mouth is foul and dry; but care must be taken that neither the pulp nor\r
#Text=the stones are swallowed.
#Text=These will both refresh and feed the patient\r
#Text=as much as is necessary until the decline of the disease.
#Text=The parent\r
#Text=must strictly forbid the attendants in the sick chamber giving, at this\r
#Text=period, any heating or stimulating fluid, as also animal food; and this\r
#Text=injunction must be strictly regarded, even in the mildest form of the\r
#Text=disease.
#Text=When the child is convalescent, mild nourishment will be required,\r
#Text=such as arrow-root, tapioca, chicken or mutton broth, beef tea,\r
#Text=jellies, and roasted apples; and by and by a mutton chop.
#Text=Wine is\r
#Text=seldom necessary, except under circumstances of unusual debility after\r
#Text=a protracted illness, when its moderate use tends much to assist the\r
#Text=convalescence; but, if given unadvisedly, there will be great hazard of\r
#Text=exciting internal inflammatory disease.
#Text=Relapses are sometimes caused by the child getting about too soon, and\r
#Text=by indulgence of the appetite, particularly for food: a proper degree\r
#Text=of restraint, therefore, must be placed upon the child by the parent,\r
#Text=who cannot too strictly carry out the directions of the medical\r
#Text=attendant upon the diet and regimen during this period.
#Text=Great attention must still be paid to the state of the bowels, and,\r
#Text=indeed, to all the secretions and excretions.
#Text=PEELING OFF OF THE CUTICLE, AND FALLING OFF OF THE HAIR.
#Text=--To promote\r
#Text=the more easy separation of the cuticle from the surface of the body, a\r
#Text=warm or tepid bath may be usefully employed at the close of the\r
#Text=disease.
#Text=It will, moreover, greatly contribute to the comfort of the\r
#Text=child, and induce a more healthy condition of the skin.
#Text=Occasionally\r
#Text=the cuticle of the whole hand and fingers will peel off unbroken, when\r
#Text=it will resemble precisely a glove in shape.
#Text=As is the case in all fevers, more or less, so particularly after\r
#Text=scarlet fever, there is a great tendency to the falling off of the\r
#Text=hair.
#Text=It will be always well, therefore, to shave the head at this\r
#Text=time, and exhibit daily a tepid shower bath, as early as the strength\r
#Text=of the child will permit.
#Text=CAUTIONS, ETC.
#Text=--The contagious character of this disease requires the\r
#Text=separation of the invalid from the rest of the family; and, when it is\r
#Text=practicable, the children should be removed to a distance.
#Text=This measure\r
#Text=is imperatively called for, when the form of the disease is very severe\r
#Text=in its character.
#Text=Great caution must also be exercised, after the convalescence of the\r
#Text=patient, that the other children are not brought into too early contact\r
#Text=with him: for infection may be thus produced, though several weeks may\r
#Text=have elapsed from the period of the peeling off of the skin.
#Text=The period at which the disease shows itself after the exposure of an\r
#Text=individual to sources of contagion, is exceedingly various.
#Text=One child\r
#Text=will be seized within a few hours; another, not for some days; and now\r
#Text=and then (though rarely), five or six weeks have intervened between the\r
#Text=period of exposure and the manifestation of the disease.
#Text=When this disease is rife in a family, it will frequently affect the\r
#Text=individuals composing it very differently.
#Text=Some escape altogether;--\r
#Text=others have the mild form of the complaint;--others the severe;--and,\r
#Text=again, the attendant in the sick room may be attacked with the sore\r
#Text=throat and fever only, both of which may subside without any appearance\r
#Text=of a rash.
#Text=In conclusion, this disease is a complaint of infancy and childhood,\r
#Text=rather than of adult age; generally affects the same individual but\r
#Text=once during his life; and, though examples of a second attack have\r
#Text=occurred, such a circumstance is extremely rare.
#Text=Sect.
#Text=VI.
#Text=--MEASLES.
#Text=Measles consists of a fever, in which the mucous lining of the air-\r
#Text=passages is principally affected, and which, after about three days'\r
#Text=duration, results in an eruption of a red rash over the surface.
#Text=It depends upon a specific contagion;--occurs most frequently during\r
#Text=childhood and adolescence, though no age is exempt from it;--and affects\r
#Text=the system but once; a peculiarity to which an exception is very rare,\r
#Text=proved by the few instances of the kind which have been recorded.
#Text=The period at which the disease manifests itself after infection is\r
#Text=various,--generally about the ninth day; it has, however, been delayed\r
#Text=until the sixteenth.
#Text=DESCRIPTION OF THE DISEASE.
#Text=--The child infected will be observed not to\r
#Text=be as well as usual, less active, and out of spirits; his appetite\r
#Text=will fail, and his sleep be restless and disturbed.
#Text=It will soon be\r
#Text=evident that he has apparently taken a cold in his head, and that this\r
#Text=is accompanied by fever.
#Text=His voice will be hoarse; there will be\r
#Text=frequent cough, headach, sneezing, running from the nose and eyes,--the\r
#Text=eyelids being somewhat swollen, and the eyes inflamed;--the skin will be\r
#Text=hot and dry, and he will complain of occasional chilliness.
#Text=In the\r
#Text=course of the next two or three days, these symptoms will increase in\r
#Text=severity, and perhaps be accompanied by oppression at the chest and\r
#Text=hurried breathing, and towards evening by slight delirium.
#Text=On the fourth day, the rash will appear, but the symptoms will be\r
#Text=little, if at all, mitigated; indeed, they will sometimes increase in\r
#Text=severity.
#Text=The eruption will first be perceived about the head and face,\r
#Text=in the form of small red spots, at first distinct from each other, but\r
#Text=soon coalescing, and forming patches of an irregular crescent-like or\r
#Text=semilunar figure, of a dull red colour, and slightly elevated (giving a\r
#Text=sensation of hardness to the finger), while portions of the skin\r
#Text=intervening between them will retain their natural appearance.
#Text=At this\r
#Text=time the eruption will also be found on the inside of the mouth and\r
#Text=throat, and the hoarseness will consequently increase.
#Text=On the fifth day, the rash usually covers the whole surface of the\r
#Text=body, with the exception of the legs and feet; and is now very vivid on\r
#Text=the face, which is not unfrequently so much swelled, especially the\r
#Text=eyelids, that the eyes are quite closed up, as in small-pox.
#Text=On the\r
#Text=sixth day, it is fully out on the extremities, and is beginning to fade\r
#Text=on the face.
#Text=On the eighth, it is fading from all parts; on the ninth,\r
#Text=it is hardly perceptible; and has entirely disappeared on the tenth day\r
#Text=from the commencement of the fever, or the sixth from its own first\r
#Text=appearance.
#Text=As the fading proceeds, the spots drop off in the form of\r
#Text=little branny scales, which are sometimes, from their minuteness,\r
#Text=scarcely perceptible.
#Text=They leave a slight discolouration on the skin,\r
#Text=with considerable itching.
#Text=Such is the ordinary course of this disease; occasionally, however,\r
#Text=deviations are met with.
#Text=CHARACTER OF MEASLES COMPARED WITH SCARLET FEVER AND SMALL-POX.
#Text=--Under\r
#Text=the description given of Scarlet Fever, are noticed several signs by\r
#Text=which that disease may be distinguished from measles: to these may be\r
#Text=added the absence of cough, of water flowing from the eyes, and of\r
#Text=redness and swelling of the eyelids as in measles.
#Text=Again, in measles,\r
#Text=the eruption is more pointed, of a crimson instead of a scarlet hue,\r
#Text=and does not appear until two days later than in scarlet fever.
#Text=In small-pox, the fever abates as soon as the eruption makes its\r
#Text=appearance.
#Text=In scarlet fever, this is by no means the case; and as\r
#Text=little so in measles: the vomiting, indeed, subsides; but the cough,\r
#Text=fever, and headach grow more violent; and the difficulty of breathing,\r
#Text=weakness of the eyes, and, indeed, all the catarrhal symptoms, remain\r
#Text=without any abatement till the eruption has all but completed its\r
#Text=course.
#Text=MATERNAL MANAGEMENT.
#Text=--Measles, in its ordinary and simple form, is a\r
#Text=mild, and by no means dangerous, disease: it is sometimes, however,\r
#Text=accompanied or immediately followed by symptoms of a very serious\r
#Text=character, and which, it is to be feared, in many instances, owe their\r
#Text=origin to the carelessness of the attendants in the sick chamber.
#Text=A\r
#Text=mother's superintendence, therefore, is much required at this time to\r
#Text=insure a careful attention to the medical directions, as also to those\r
#Text=general points of management upon which the well-doing of her child\r
#Text=much depend, of which the following are the most important:--\r
#Text=\r
#Text=\r
#Text=VENTILATION OF THE BED-ROOM, ETC.
#Text=--The child must be kept in bed from\r
#Text=the onset of the attack.
#Text=He must have so much clothing only as will\r
#Text=secure his comfort, avoiding equally too much heat or exposure to cold.
#Text=To these points the parent's attention must be particularly directed.
#Text=It is the practice with some nurses, in the belief that a breath of\r
#Text=cool air is most pernicious, to keep the child constantly enveloped in\r
#Text=a smothering heap of bed-clothes, with curtains closely drawn, and the\r
#Text=room well heated by fire, by which means the fever and all its\r
#Text=concomitant dangers are greatly augmented.
#Text=It is equally a popular\r
#Text=error (and yet by many it is still held and acted upon) to suppose that\r
#Text=because in small-pox exposure to cold is useful, that therefore it\r
#Text=must be of equal advantage in measles.
#Text=It cannot be too generally known\r
#Text=that the nature of the fevers accompanying the two diseases are widely\r
#Text=different, and that the adoption of this error is productive of the\r
#Text=most serious consequences; for it would most likely produce in measles\r
#Text=inflammation of the lungs, which, in truth, is commonly the result of\r
#Text=carelessness upon this point.
#Text=The bed-room should be large and airy; free from currents of cold, but\r
#Text=well ventilated, and not hot.
#Text=The room, also, must be darkened, on\r
#Text=account of the tenderness of the eyes; all noise excluded, and mental\r
#Text=excitement or irritation carefully avoided.
#Text=REGIMEN.
#Text=--Little or no food must be allowed, and whatever is taken must\r
#Text=be of the simplest kind, and in a liquid form.
#Text=Mild mucilaginous\r
#Text=drinks, and warm, may be given liberally; as barley-water, or thin\r
#Text=gruel, etc.
#Text=SPONGING, ETC.
#Text=--The face, chest, arms, and hands should be sponged\r
#Text=occasionally with vinegar and warm water (one fourth and three\r
#Text=fourths).
#Text=This will be productive of great comfort to the little\r
#Text=patient; it removes the heat, dryness, and itching of the skin, which\r
#Text=are often very distressing; and is especially useful at night in\r
#Text=relieving wakefulness.
#Text=If the cough be troublesome, it will be useful\r
#Text=for the child to breathe the steam of warm water; not through an\r
#Text=inhaler, but over a large basin, with the head covered with flannel\r
#Text=large enough to hang over its edges.
#Text=By this means the tender and\r
#Text=inflamed eyes will at the same time derive advantage from the soothing\r
#Text=effect of the vapour.
#Text=CAUTIONS.
#Text=--Whenever the measles is known to be prevalent in a\r
#Text=neighbourhood, and a child manifests symptoms of cold in the head and\r
#Text=fever, it should at once be a reason for carefulness on the part of the\r
#Text=parent.
#Text=The diet should be light, cooling, and scanty; and the child\r
#Text=should be carefully kept in doors.
#Text=It has been before remarked, that in its ordinary course measles is a\r
#Text=disease unaccompanied with danger, but that the mildest form may be\r
#Text=speedily converted into the most dangerous.
#Text=That is to say, a sudden\r
#Text=change may lake place in the symptoms, arising out of circumstances\r
#Text=which could not have been foreseen, and therefore unavoidable; or may\r
#Text=be produced by improper management on the part of the nurse, such as\r
#Text=the giving of stimulants, by too much heat, or by exposure to cold.
#Text=Now\r
#Text=it is for the parent early to notice any change which may occur from\r
#Text=the first source, and by her watchfulness to guard against the\r
#Text=possibility of its arising from either of the second.
#Text=In reference to the first, if the child should complain at any period\r
#Text=of the disease of severe headach, with piercing pain through the\r
#Text=temples, and if this is accompanied by wandering of mind, great\r
#Text=increase of suffusion of the eyes, as also intolerance of light, the\r
#Text=immediate attention of the medical man is demanded.
#Text=So, if towards the\r
#Text=dose of the eruption, that is, from the seventh to the ninth day, the\r
#Text=breathing should again become hurried (this symptom is very generally\r
#Text=present during the height of the eruption, and is not necessarily\r
#Text=connected with disease of the lungs), with pain and oppression felt at\r
#Text=the chest, the cough becoming hacking and incessant, etc.
#Text=(all symptoms\r
#Text=cognizable by the mother, and indicative of inflammation of the\r
#Text=lungs), no time must be lost in seeking medical aid.
#Text=With regard to the last cause (improper management), it may be well,\r
#Text=in reference to it, to observe, that it sometimes happens that the rash\r
#Text=comes out imperfectly, or, having appeared properly, suddenly\r
#Text=retrocedes and disappears; and that under such circumstances the nurse\r
#Text=will almost certainly, if not well watched, give the child "a good\r
#Text=dose of sulphur in diluted spirit, or a glass of punch containing\r
#Text=saffron," which are considered specifics for bringing out the eruption.
#Text=Nothing can be more injurious than such remedies, for generally the\r
#Text=disappearance of the rash will be dependent upon the existence of some\r
#Text=internal inflammation, or of too high a fever; for the removal of\r
#Text=which the medical man ought to be instantly applied to.
#Text=Sometimes,\r
#Text=however, it may be fairly traced to a careless exposure to cold: under\r
#Text=such circumstances the child should be instantly, and without\r
#Text=hesitation, put into the warm bath.
#Text=Measles are frequently followed by cough, and deranged bowels; and\r
#Text=there is always great susceptibility about the child for some time.
#Text=On\r
#Text=this account he should be carefully screened from a cold or damp\r
#Text=atmosphere; the diet should be carefully regulated; and flannel worn\r
#Text=next to the skin.
#Text=If the cough should continue, it must not be\r
#Text=neglected on the supposition that it will wear off; for it demands the\r
#Text=skilful and careful attention of the medical man.
