#Text=In conclusion, it may be remarked that very frequently during infancy\r
#Text=and childhood, and particularly during the period of teething,\r
#Text=eruptions very similar in appearance to this disease occur; unless,\r
#Text=however, they are accompanied by the specific fever, and run the\r
#Text=regular course, they may at once be decided upon as not being the\r
#Text=measles.
#Text=Sect.
#Text=VII.
#Text=--SMALL-POX.
#Text=This disease, the most dreaded of all eruptive fevers, is not so\r
#Text=commonly met with in the present day as formerly; thanks to that\r
#Text=Providence which led to the discovery of Jenner.
#Text=But although its\r
#Text=occurrence is not so frequent, it still does occasionally present\r
#Text=itself; when it will assume either a mild or severe form.
#Text=If it attack\r
#Text=a child that has not previously been vaccinated, it is called natural\r
#Text=small-pox; and the chances are that the disorder will be severe in\r
#Text=character;--if, on the other hand, it occur in the vaccinated, the\r
#Text=disease will generally be much modified in its symptoms; the attack\r
#Text=will be mild, and without danger.
#Text=NATURAL SMALL-POX.
#Text=--The infection of small-pox having been received\r
#Text=into the system of a child that has not been vaccinated, fourteen days\r
#Text=(on an average) will transpire before the commencement of the febrile\r
#Text=symptoms, or eruptive fever.
#Text=A distinct rigor or shivering fit then\r
#Text=takes place, accompanied by pain in the back or in the stomach, with\r
#Text=sickness, giddiness, or headach; as also great drowsiness.
#Text=And if an\r
#Text=infant be the subject of the disease, a convulsive fit will sometimes\r
#Text=take place, or several in succession.
#Text=At the end of eight-and-forty hours from the occurrence of the rigor\r
#Text=(in the majority of cases), the eruption comes out; and shows itself\r
#Text=first on the face and neck in minute flea-bite spots.
#Text=In the course of\r
#Text=the next four-and-twenty hours in some cases, and in others not until\r
#Text=the expiration of two or three days, it completely covers the body; not\r
#Text=being confined exclusively to the skin, but frequently extending to the\r
#Text=mouth and throat, and even to the external membrane of the eye.
#Text=In the course of two or three days from their first appearance the\r
#Text=little pimples, increasing in size, will be found to contain a thin\r
#Text=transparent fluid, to pit or become depressed in their centre, and the\r
#Text=skin in the spaces between them will be found red.
#Text=On the seventh or\r
#Text=eighth day from the commencement of the fever, the fluid contained in\r
#Text=the pimples will be no longer transparent, but opaque; and they will\r
#Text=consequently appear white, or of a light straw colour.
#Text=Each pimple or\r
#Text=pock will be no longer depressed in its centre, but will become raised\r
#Text=and pointed, being more fully distended by the increased quantity of\r
#Text=fluid within; and the skin around each pock will now be of a bright\r
#Text=crimson.
#Text=The head, face, hands, and wherever else the eruption shows\r
#Text=itself, gradually swell; and the eyelids are often so much distended as\r
#Text=to close the eyes and produce temporary blindness.
#Text=There will always at\r
#Text=this time be some degree of fever present, and its amount will vary\r
#Text=with the circumstances of each individual ease.
#Text=The skin too will be\r
#Text=very tender, so much so sometimes as greatly to harass and distress the\r
#Text=child.
#Text=On the eleventh day the swelling and inflam of the skin of the body\r
#Text=and face subside; the pimples upon these parts dry up and form scabs,\r
#Text=which fall off about the fourteenth or fifteenth day.
#Text=Those on the\r
#Text=hands, as they come out later, commonly continue a short time longer.
#Text=The eruption leaves behind, in some cases, the peculiar marks of the\r
#Text=disease; and in others merely discoloured spots, which disappear in the\r
#Text=progress of a short time.
#Text=The natural small-pox is sometimes much more severe in its character\r
#Text=than the foregoing, and what is called confluent small-pox is said to\r
#Text=exist.
#Text=This form will be marked by great constitutional disturbance,\r
#Text=and the eruption coming out earlier than in the milder form; instead of\r
#Text=being distinct, that is, each pimple standing distinct and separate\r
#Text=one from the other, they will coalesce, and appear flat and doughy, not\r
#Text=prominent: they will more particularly run into each other on the face,\r
#Text=where they will form one continuous bag, which soon becoming a sore,\r
#Text=will discharge copiously.
#Text=SMALL-POX IN THE VACCINATED.
#Text=--When small-pox occurs to those that have\r
#Text=been formerly vaccinated, the disease, in almost every instance, is\r
#Text=much altered or modified in its character.
#Text=Indeed in children, in whom\r
#Text=of course vaccination has been but comparatively lately performed,\r
#Text=small-pox when it occurs will, in the majority of cases, be so mild\r
#Text=that the real nature of the disease will be with difficulty determined:\r
#Text=so mild, that again and again has a parent been heard to exclaim,\r
#Text="Surely these few scattered pimples cannot be the small-pox!"
#Text=If,\r
#Text=however, as the pimples progress, they are narrowly watched, and are\r
#Text=seen to become depressed in their centre; if there has been the\r
#Text=precursory rigor, etc.; and if the source of the disorder can be traced\r
#Text=to some case of undoubted small-pox, the child in fact having been\r
#Text=exposed to contagion, no doubt ought to exist in reference to the\r
#Text=nature of such a case, however slight may be the character of the\r
#Text=disease.
#Text=The usual progress, however, of small-pox modified by vaccination is\r
#Text=as follows.
#Text=The first stage is the same usually as in the natural form\r
#Text=of the disease.
#Text=As soon, however, as the eruption appears, the\r
#Text=modifying power of the vaccination becomes apparent.
#Text=The eruption will\r
#Text=be found to be generally both less in quantity and more limited in its\r
#Text=extent; or if even it should come out profusely, and cover a large\r
#Text=extent of the surface of the body, still the controuling power of the\r
#Text=vaccination will immediately show itself after its appearance,--first,\r
#Text=in the complete subsidence of all the febrile symptoms which will now\r
#Text=take place; and, secondly, in reference to the eruption, part of which\r
#Text=will die away at once, and the remainder will by the fifth day be\r
#Text=filled with the opaque yellowish fluid, then dry up, becoming hard and\r
#Text=horny, and falling off will leave a mottled red appearance of the skin,\r
#Text=and now and then slight pitting.
#Text=Such is the usual progress of the disease: subsequent to vaccination,\r
#Text=it is a mild and tractable disorder.
#Text=It is right, however, to mention\r
#Text=that small-pox has occurred even to the vaccinated in almost as severe\r
#Text=a form as the confluent natural small-pox, and running its regular\r
#Text=course unaltered or unmodified.
#Text=Such instances, however, are extremely\r
#Text=rare, and form the exceptions to the general rule; for "no reasonable\r
#Text=doubt can be entertained, from the abundance of facts now before the\r
#Text=world, that such modification is the law of the animal economy, and\r
#Text=that the regular or natural progress is the exception."
